responseBody.name: Citizenship in the Community
responseBody.searchKeywords: Citizenship in the Community Merit Badge Requirements

ranks.name: Scout
ranks.header: All requirements for the Scout rank must be completed as a member  of a troop or as a Lone Scout. If you have already completed these requirements as part of the Webelos Scouting Adventure, simply demonstrate your knowledge or skills to your Scoutmaster or other designated leader after joining the troop.
ranks.footer:
NOTES: The requirements for Scout, Tenderfoot, Second Class, and First Class ranks may be worked on simultaneously; however, these ranks must be earned in sequence.

Alternative requirements for the Scout rank are available for Scouts with physical or mental disabilities if they meet the criteria listed in the Scouts BSA Requirements book.

1 If your family does not have internet access at home AND you do not have ready internet access at school or another public place or via a mobile device, the Personal Safety Awareness videos portion of this requirement may be waived by your Scoutmaster in consultation with your parent or guardian.
ranks.searchKeywords: Boy Scout Scouting Scout Rank Award Requirements

ranks.name: Eagle Scout
ranks.header:
IMPORTANT: The 2022 version of the Scouts BSA Eagle Rank has been added to Scoutbook. This version requires the Citizenship in Society Merit Badge to be completed in order to complete Requirement 3 (Merit Badges) and the Eagle Rank.

If a Scout will complete all Eagle requirements except the Eagle Board of Review prior to July 1, 2022, the Eagle rank can be manually switched back to the 2016 version. To do this, click on 2016 above.
ranks.footer:
NOTES:
For Venturers working on Scouts BSA requirements, replace "troop" with "crew" and "Scoutmaster" with "crew Advisor." For Sea Scouts working on Scouts BSA requirements, replace "troop" with "ship" and "Scoutmaster" with "Skipper."

11Assistant patrol leader and bugler are not approved positions of responsibility for the Eagle Scout rank. Likewise, a Scoutmaster-approved leadership project cannot be used in lieu of serving in a position of responsibility.

12 Appeals and Extensions
  • If a Scout believes all requirements for the Eagle Scout rank have been completed but a board of review is denied, the Scout may request a board of review under disputed circumstances in accordance with Guide to Advancement topic 8.0.3.
  • If the board of review does not approve the Scout’s advancement, the decision may be appealed in accordance with Guide to Advancement topic 8.0.4.0.
  • A Scout who foresees that, due to no fault or choice of their own, it will not be possible to complete the Eagle Scout rank requirements before age 18 may apply for a limited time extension in accordance with Guide to Advancement topic 9.0.4.0. These are rarely granted and reserved only for work on Eagle.

13 Age Requirement Eligibility Merit badges and badges of rank may be earned by a registered Scout or a qualified Venturer or Sea Scout. Scouts may earn these awards until their 18th birthday. Any Venturer or Sea Scout who has achieved the First Class rank as a Scout in a troop or as a Lone Scout may continue working up to their 18th birthday toward the Star, Life, and Eagle Scout ranks.

An Eagle Scout board of review may occur, without special approval, within 24 months after the 18th birthday. Consult the Guide to Advancement topic 8.0.3.1, if a Board of Review is to be conducted more than 24 months after the candidate’s 18th birthday.

If you have a permanent physical or mental disability, or a disability expected to last more than two years or beyond age 18, you may become an Eagle Scout by qualifying for as many required merit badges as you can and qualifying for alternative merit badges for the rest. If you seek to become an Eagle Scout under this procedure, you must submit a special application to your local council service center. Your application must be approved by your council advancement committee before you can work on alternative merit badges.

A Scout, Venturer, or Sea Scout with a disability may also qualify to work toward rank advancement after reaching 18 years of age if the guidelines outlined in section 10 of the Guide to Advancement are met.
ranks.searchKeywords: Boy Scout Scouting Eagle Rank Award Requirements

ranks.name: Life Scout
ranks.footer:
NOTES: For Venturers working on Scouts BSA requirements, replace "troop" with "crew" and "Scoutmaster" with "crew Advisor." For Sea Scouts working on Scouts BSA requirements, replace "troop" with "ship" and "Scoutmaster" with "Skipper."

9 Assistant patrol leader is not an approved position of responsibility for the Star, Life, or Eagle rank.

10 If the board of review does not approve the Scout's advancement, the decision may be appealed in accordance with Guide to Advancement topic 8.0.4.0.
ranks.searchKeywords: Boy Scout Scouting Life Scout Rank Award Requirements

ranks.name: Star Scout
ranks.footer:
NOTES: For Venturers working on Scouts BSA requirements, replace "troop" with "crew" and "Scoutmaster" with "Crew Advisor." For Sea Scouts working on Scouts BSA requirements, replace "troop" with "ship" and "Scoutmaster" with "Skipper."

6 Assistant patrol leader is not an approved position of responsibility for the Star, Life, or Eagle rank.

7 If your family does not have internet access at home AND you do not have ready internet access at school or another public place or via a mobile device, the Cyber Chip portion of this requirement may be waived by your Scoutmaster in consultation with your parent or guardian.

8 If the board of review does not approve the Scout's advancement, the decision may be appealed in accordance with Guide to Advancement topic 8.0.4.0.
ranks.searchKeywords: Boy Scout Scouting Star Scout Rank Award Requirements

ranks.name: First Class
ranks.footer:
NOTES: The requirements for Scout, Tenderfoot, Second Class, and First Class ranks may be worked on simultaneously; however, these ranks must be earned in sequence.

Alternative requirements for the First Class rank are available for Scouts with physical or mental disabilities if they meet the criteria listed in the Scout BSA Requirements book.

4 See the Swimming merit badge requirements for details about the BSA swimmer test.

5 Under certain exceptional conditions, where the climate keeps the outdoor water temperature below safe levels yearround, or where there are no suitably safe and accessible places (outdoors or indoors) within a reasonable traveling distance to swim at any time during the year, the council Scout executive and advancement committee may, on an individual Scout basis, authorize an alternative for requirements 6a and 6e. The local council may establish appropriate procedures for submitting and processing these types of requests. All the other requirements, none of which necessitate entry in the water or entry in a watercraft on the water, must be completed as written.
ranks.searchKeywords: Boy Scout Scouting First Class Rank Award Requirements

ranks.name: Second Class
ranks.footer:
NOTES: The requirements for Scout, Tenderfoot, Second Class, and First Class ranks may be worked on simultaneously; however, these ranks must be earned in sequence.

Alternative requirements for the Second Class rank are available for Scouts with physical or mental disabilities if they meet the criteria listed in the Scouts BSA Requirements book.

2 If you use a wheelchair or crutches, or if it is difficult for you to get around, you may substitute trip for hike in requirement 3b and 3c.

3 Under certain exceptional conditions, where the climate keeps the outdoor water temperature below safe levels yearround, or where there are no suitably safe and accessible places (outdoors or indoors) within a reasonable traveling distance to swim at any time during the year, the council Scout executive and advancement committee may, on an individual Scout basis, authorize an alternative for requirements 5b and 5c. The local council may establish appropriate procedures for submitting and processing these types of requests. All the other requirements, none of which necessitate entry in the water or entry in a watercraft on the water, must be completed as written.
ranks.searchKeywords: Boy Scout Scouting Second Class Rank Award Requirements

Alternative requirements for the Scout rank are available for Scouts with physical or mental disabilities if they meet the criteria listed in the Scouts BSA Requirements book.

requirements.name: Present yourself to your leader, prepared for an overnight camping trip. Show the personal and camping gear you will use. Show the right way to pack and carry it.

Record your best in the following tests:

Current results
  • Push-ups ________ (Record the number done correctly in 60 seconds)
  • Sit-ups or curl-ups________ (Record the number done correctly in 60 seconds)
  • Back-saver sit-and-reach _________ (Record the distance stretched)
  • 1 mile walk/run _____________ (record the time)
Describe common poisonous or hazardous plants; identify any that grow in your local area or campsite location. Tell how to treat for exposure to them.

Show first aid for the following:
  • Simple cuts and scrapes
  • Blisters on the hand and foot
  • Minor (thermal/heat) burns or scalds (superficial, or first-degree)
  • Bites or stings of insects and ticks
  • Venomous snakebite
  • Nosebleed
  • Frostbite and sunburn
  • Choking
Demonstrate Scout spirit by living the Scout Oath and Scout Law. Tell how you have done your duty to God and how you have lived four different points of the Scout Law in your everyday life.
While working toward the Tenderfoot rank, and after completing Scout rank requirement 7, participate in a Scoutmaster conference.
Successfully complete your board of review for the Tenderfoot rank.
Spend at least one night on a patrol or troop campout. Sleep in a tent you have helped pitch.
On the campout, assist in preparing one of the meals. Tell why it is important for each patrol member to share in meal preparation and cleanup.
While on a campout, demonstrate an appropriate method of safely cleaning items used to prepare, serve, and eat a meal.
Demonstrate a practical use of two half-hitches.
Demonstrate a practical use of the square knot.
Explain the rules of safe and responsible hiking, both on the highway and cross-country, during the day and at night.
Demonstrate how to display, raise, lower, and fold the American flag.

Explain how you demonstrated the Outdoor Code and Leave No Trace on
campouts or outings.
Explain the importance of eating together as a patrol.
Demonstrate a practical use of the taut-line hitch.
Demonstrate proper care, sharpening, and use of the knife, saw, and ax. Describe when each should be used.
Tell what you can do while on a campout or other outdoor activity to prevent or reduce the occurrence of injuries or exposure listed in Tenderfoot requirements 4a and 4b.
Explain the importance of the buddy system as it relates to your personal safety on outings and where you live. Use the buddy system while on a troop or patrol outing.
Assemble a personal first-aid kit to carry with you on future campouts and hikes. Tell how each item in the kit would be used.
Describe what to do if you become lost on a hike or campout.
Develop and describe a plan for improvement in each of the activities listed in Tenderfoot requirement 6a. Keep track of your activity for at least 30 days.

Show improvement (of any degree) in each activity listed in Tenderfoot requirement 6a after practicing for 30 days.

30 days later
  • Push-ups ________ (Record the number done correctly in 60 seconds)
  • Sit-ups or curl-ups________ (Record the number done correctly in 60 seconds)
  • Back-saver sit-and-reach _________ (Record the distance stretched)
  • 1 mile walk/run _____________ (record the time)
Participate in a total of one hour of service in one or more service projects approved by your Scoutmaster. Explain how your service to others relates to the Scout slogan and Scout motto.
Describe the steps in Scouting's EDGE method.  Use the Teaching EDGE method to teach another person how to tie the square knot.
Explain why it is important to hike on trails or other durable surfaces, and  give examples of durable surfaces you saw on your outing.
requirements.short: Why it is important to hike on trails

responseBody.name: Campout Charlie Elliot Wildlife Center (Fishing)
responseBody.description:
The best fishing access for a large group in the state.The Activity: Fishing at Marben PFA (Charlie Elliott Wildlife Center). 22 managed ponds for bass/bream.

https://georgiawildlife.com/marben-pfa

Bonus: Jon Wood Astronomy Field is on-site for star parties.The Camping: Charlie Elliott Campground. Cleaner and quieter than most state parks. Central shower house.Drive Time: ~1 hour.

responseBody.name: Board of Review
responseBody.description:
oard of Review will be held after the regular troop meeting if there are at least 2 adult board members and at least one scout who has RSVPed to sit for a board of review. If no scouts have RSVPed by the day before the meeting then the BoR may be canceled.

Scouts should know what rank(s), Merit Badge(s) they are earning and be prepared to speak about their experience earning them.

responseBody.name: Board of Review
responseBody.description:

requestBody.name: Lake Burton Campout & Eagle Project Support
requestBody.description:
Friday night campout at Lake Burton with an opportunity Saturday morning to support Charlie Brunt's Eagle Scout project. Charlie is giving a presentation on Electric Shock Drowning (ESD) awareness and prevention at a community meeting (10:00 AM, location TBD) and distributing dock warning signs and emergency power disconnect stickers.

This event is tentative - we need a second registered adult to make it official.

responseBody.name: Merit Badge Clinic: FAMILY LIFE
responseBody.description:
Family Life is an EAGLE-REQUIRED merit badge.

As your Advancement Chair, I highly recommend you sign your Scout up for this if they haven't started the MB already. Especially our newest Scouts if it works with your schedule. If it doesn't, I can offer it again next school year.

Only a few requirements are covered in the session, but once the Scouts have started this MB with a counselor, they can work on the rest of the requirements on their own schedule over the years. This would be the same anywhere because, for example, you have to track your chores over a 3-month period.

Nicole Allen is the Merit Badge Counselor for this MB. The clinic will be held at her house, in her basement where the movie projector is so they can see the powerpoint presentation. Parents are welcome to observe if they wish. We do need a 2nd adult to stay for the "2-deep" safety coverage, so please let Nicole know if you plan to hang out anyway.

This clinic is scheduled for 1.5 hours but may end early so please keep yourself available to pick up between 11:30-12pm if your Scout texts to say we're finishing up. They are also welcome to stay through to 12pm of course; but I'm guessing they'd prefer to leave when the class ends.

PREREQUISITES: Prior to the MB clinic, each Scout Scouts should come prepared with the answer to Requirement #1 and Requirement #2. Scouts should come prepared with the answer to both items hand written down on paper. You can find the merit badge requirements online at this link:

https://www.scouting.org/merit-badges/family-life/

oard of Review will be held after the regular troop meeting if there are at least 2 adult board members and at least one scout who has RSVPed to sit for a board of review. If no scouts have RSVPed by the day before the meeting then the BoR may be canceled.

responseBody.name: Eagle Meritbadge Summit@KSU
responseBody.description:
Eagle Merit Badge Summit Saturday, February 7th / KSU Marietta Campus  / 7:00am-5:30pm Registration Opens: Dec 8th 9:00am

Check-in: 7:00am-7:30am for morning/all day classes and 12:00pm-12:30pm for afternoon sessions

Note that we will NOT be posting merit badge requirements until beginning of 2026 as National makes updates to various badges each year.

All classes will be 4 hours in length except for Camping and First Aid. Classes will usually be offered either from 8 AM  12 PM or 1:00 PM  5:00PM. If you are staying all day, please bring a lunch or purchase a Chick-Fil-A lunch for $13 from the class section in your registration. The lunch option will close February 2nd.

activities.name: Day of Fire Service for Atlanta Area Council
activities.description: The annual "Day of Fire" is a CAAB tradition where scouts burn driftwood for 12 hours. 2026 date is January 17th. Scouts can earn three conservation service hours or stay the entire 12 hours. We also repurpose Christmas Trees as fish habitats.

activities.name: Campout - Allatoona Aquatics Base
activities.description:
Atlanta Area Council's Day of Fire will be Saturday January 17th. Pending reasonable winter temperatures, we will camp at the Scout's Allatoona Aquatics Base on Friday night January 17th. On Saturday we will participate with the Day of Fire and then head home afterwards. https://www.scoutingatl.org/DayOfFire

Note: there are no showers on the base. We will strip off muddy clothes before the drive home.

responseBody.name: Campout - Allatoona Aquatics Base
responseBody.description:

On Saturday we will participate with the Day of Fire and then head home afterwards. https://www.scoutingatl.org/DayOfFire

Note: there are no showers on the base. We will strip off muddy clothes before the drive home.

responseBody.name: Service - Day of Fire for Atlanta Area Council
responseBody.description: Scouts can work whatever hours they wish. Up to 3 hours can be counted as Conservation Service Hours.
